{{Short description|none}} <!-- This short description is INTENTIONALLY "none" - please see WP:SDNONE before you consider changing it! -->
The '''psychology of music preference''' is the study of the [[psychology|psychological factors]] behind peoples' different music preferences. One study found that after researching through studies from the past 50 years, there are more than 500 functions for music.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schäfer |first1=Thomas |last2=Sedlmeier |first2=Peter |last3=Städtler |first3=Christine |last4=Huron |first4=David |date=2013 |title=The psychological functions of music listening |journal=Frontiers in Psychology |volume=4 |page=511 |doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00511 |doi-access=free |pmid=23964257 |pmc=3741536 |issn=1664-1078}}</ref> Music is heard by people daily in many parts of the world, and affects people in various ways from emotional regulation to cognitive development, along with providing a means for self-expression. Music training has been shown to help improve intellectual development and ability, though minimal connection has been found as to how it affects emotion regulation.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Schellenberg|first=Glen E.|author2=Mankarious, Monika |title=Music training and emotion comprehension in childhood|journal=Emotion|date=October 2012|volume=12|issue=5|pages=887–891|doi=10.1037/a0027971|pmid=22642351}}</ref> Numerous studies have been conducted to show that individual personality can have an effect on music preference, though a recent meta-analysis has shown that personality in itself explains little variance in music preferences.<ref name=":3"/> These studies are not limited to American culture, as they have been conducted with significant results in countries all over the world, including Japan,<ref name="Brown 18"/> Germany,<ref name="Langmeyer rudan tarnai"/> Spain,<ref name="premuzic furnham freixanet muro"/> and Brazil.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Herrera|first1=Lucia|last2=Soares-Quadros|first2=João F. Jr|last3=Lorenzo|first3=Oswaldo|date=2018|title=Music Preferences and Personality in Brazilians|journal=Frontiers in Psychology|language=en|volume=9|page=1488|doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2018.01488|issn=1664-1078|pmc=6113570|pmid=30186197|doi-access=free}}</ref>

== Personality and music preference ==

===Personality===
The relationship between musical preference and personality has remained a long-standing topic of contention for researchers due to the variability in results and the low-predictive power that personality has demonstrated on music preferences.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last1=Schäfer |first1=Thomas|last2=Mehlhorn|first2=Claudia|date=29 April 2017|title=Can personality traits predict musical style preferences? A meta-analysis|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0191886917303215|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|volume=116|pages=265–273|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2017.04.061|url-access=subscription}}</ref> A meta-analysis conducted by Schäfer and Mehlorn, (2017) of previous studies trying to determine if experience seeking or any of the [[Big Five personality traits|Big-Five personality traits]] predicted musical preferences, revealed that the correlation coefficient between music genre and personality traits possessed a magnitude greater than 0.1 in only 6 out of the 30 studies they reviewed. Openness to experience was found to have the strongest correlation with a preference for three musical styles; however, this correlation was still relatively minor. The vast majority of the correlation coefficients were almost zero.<ref name=":3" />

Recent research has used data from music streaming sites instead of self-reported data to show that music genres and the user's listening habits can be used to predict the Big Five personality traits.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last1=Anderson |first1=Ian |last2=Gil |first2=Santiago |last3=Gibson |first3=Clay |last4=Wolf |first4=Scott |last5=Shapiro |first5=Will |last6=Semerci |first6=Oguz |last7=Greenberg |first7=David M. |date=May 2021 |title="Just the Way You Are": Linking Music Listening on Spotify and Personality |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1948550620923228 |journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science |language=en |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=561–572 |doi=10.1177/1948550620923228 |s2cid=233194549 |issn=1948-5506|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In a study conducted in 2020, researchers analyzed data from [[Spotify]] users and mapped the Big Five personality traits to different genres, moods, and derived metrics such as the number of playlists, diversity in song choice, and repetition of listening habits. Results showed an increase in correlation. Openness had a positive correlation with [[Atmospheric music|atmospheric]], [[reggae]], and [[Folk music|folk]] music, while conscientiousness had a negative correlation with [[Rock music|rock]] and "Energizing" songs such as "[[Happy (Pharrell Williams song)|Happy]]" by [[Pharrell Williams]].<ref name=":5" /> In terms of derived metrics, emotional stability had a negative correlation with the listener's skip rate, and openness had a positive correlation with their song discovery rate.<ref name=":5" />

One shortcoming in attempting to establish a predictive relationship between music and personality is likely a result of researchers' tendency to utilize overly homogenized musical genres, failing to account for the variance within musical genres.<ref name=":02">{{Cite journal|last1=Brisson|first1=Romain|last2=Bianchi|first2=Renzo|title=On the relevance of music genre-based analysis in research on musical tastes|journal=Psychology of Music|year=2020|volume=48|issue=6|pages=777–794|doi=10.1177/0305735619828810|s2cid=151126547}}</ref> In a study conducted by Brisson and Bianchi (2021), participants were provided a musical taste inventory and asked to rate their musical genre and sub-genre preferences. For example, [[electronic music]] is considered a genre and [[house music]] is a sub-genre of electronic music. Results indicated a high degree of variance between participant genre and sub-genre preferences. Enjoying one genre or sub-genre within a broader genre category often failed to consistently predict ratings for related genres and sub-genres with another related genre or sub-genre. Ultimately, only two consistent associations between genres and sub-genres were found, calling into question the reliability of musical genres in research.<ref name=":02" /> However, the investigation into this relationship between the influence of personality on music preference remains ongoing despite these genre-based limitations in methodology and past discrepancies in research results.

Various questionnaires have been created to both measure the big five personality traits and musical preferences. The majority of studies attempting to find the correlation between personality and musical preferences administered questionnaires to measure both traits.<ref name=":0" /><ref name="Zwiegenhaft">{{cite journal|last=Zweigenhaft|first=Richard L.|title=A Do Re Mi Encore|journal=Journal of Individual Differences|date=1 January 2008|volume=29|issue=1|pages=45–55|doi=10.1027/1614-0001.29.1.45}}</ref><ref name="premuzic furnham freixanet muro">{{cite journal|last=Chamorro-Premuzic|first=Tomas|author2=Gomà-i-Freixanet, Montserrat |author3=Furnham, Adrian |author4= Muro, Anna |title=Personality, self-estimated intelligence, and uses of music: A Spanish replication and extension using structural equation modeling.|journal=Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts|date=1 January 2009|volume=3|issue=3|pages=149–155|doi=10.1037/a0015342}}</ref><ref name="premuzic fagan furnham">{{cite journal|last=Chamorro-Premuzic|first=Tomas|author2=Fagan, Patrick |author3=Furnham, Adrian |title=Personality and uses of music as predictors of preferences for music consensually classified as happy, sad, complex, and social.|journal=Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts|date=1 January 2010|volume=4|issue=4|pages=205–213|doi=10.1037/a0019210}}</ref><ref name="Premuzic Swami Furnham Maakip">{{cite journal|last=Chamorro-Premuzic|first=Tomas|author2=Swami, Viren|author3=Furnham, Adrian|author4=Maakip, Ismail|date=1 January 2009|title=The Big Five Personality Traits and Uses of Music|journal=Journal of Individual Differences|volume=30|issue=1|pages=20–27|doi=10.1027/1614-0001.30.1.20}}</ref><ref name="Kopacz">{{cite journal|last=Kopacz|first=Malgorzata|title=Personality and music preferences: The influence of personality traits on preferences regarding musical elements|journal=Journal of Music Therapy|year=2005|volume=42|issue=3|pages=216–239|doi=10.1093/jmt/42.3.216|pmid=16086606}}</ref><ref name="Langmeyer rudan tarnai">{{cite journal|last=Langmeyer|first=Alexandra|author2=Guglhör-Rudan, Angelika |author3=Tarnai, Christian |title=What Do Music Preferences Reveal About Personality?|journal=Journal of Individual Differences|date=1 January 2012|volume=33|issue=2|pages=119–130|doi=10.1027/1614-0001/a000082}}</ref><ref name="Rentfrow gosling 2003">{{cite journal|last=Rentfrow|first=Peter J.|author2=Gosling, Samuel D. |title=The do re mi's of everyday life: The structure and personality correlates of music preferences.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=1 January 2003|volume=84|issue=6|pages=1236–1256|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.84.6.1236|pmid=12793587|s2cid=16489081}}</ref> Others used questionnaires to determine personality traits, and then asked participants to rate musical excerpts on scales such as liking, perceived complexity, emotions felt, and more.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Bonneville-Roussy|first1=Arielle|last2=Rentfrow|first2=Peter J.|last3=Xu|first3=Man K.|last4=Potter|first4=Jeff|title=Music through the ages: Trends in musical engagement and preferences from adolescence through middle adulthood.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|language=en|volume=105|issue=4|pages=703–717|doi=10.1037/a0033770|pmid=23895269|year=2013}}</ref><ref name="Ladinig schellenberg">{{cite journal|last=Ladinig|first=Olivia|author2=Schellenberg, E. Glenn |title=Liking unfamiliar music: Effects of felt emotion and individual differences.|journal=Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts|date=1 January 2012|volume=6|issue=2|pages=146–154|doi=10.1037/a0024671|s2cid=56162148}}</ref><ref name="tekman hortacsu">{{cite journal|last=Tekman|first=Hasan Gürkan|author2=Hortaçsu, Nuran|title=Music and social identity: Stylistic identification as a response to musical style|journal=International Journal of Psychology|date=1 October 2002|volume=37|issue=5|pages=277–285|doi=10.1080/00207590244000043|hdl=11511/65613|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Vuoskoski thompson"/>

In general, the plasticity traits (openness to experience and extraversion) affect music preference more than the stability traits (agreeableness, neuroticism, and conscientiousness),<ref>{{cite journal|last=Miranda|first=Dave |author2=Morizot, Julien |author3=Gaudreau, Patrick|title=Personality Metatraits and Music Preferences in Adolescence: A Pilot Study|journal=International Journal of Adolescence and Youth|date=27 March 2012|volume=15|issue=4|pages=289–301|doi=10.1080/02673843.2010.9748036|s2cid=145681242 }}</ref> but each trait is still worth discussing. The personality traits have also been shown to correlate significantly with the emotional effect music has on people. Individual personality differences can help predict the emotional intensity and valence derived from music.<ref name="Vuoskoski">{{cite journal|last=Vuoskoski|first=Jonna K.|author2=Eerola, Tuomas |title=Measuring music-induced emotion: A comparison of emotion models, personality biases, and intensity of experiences|journal=Musicae Scientiae|date=13 July 2011|volume=15|issue=2|pages=159–173|doi=10.1177/1029864911403367|s2cid=144079608}}</ref>

=== Big Five personality traits ===
{{Main|Big Five personality traits}}

==== Openness to experience ====
Of all the traits, openness to experience has been shown to have the greatest effect upon genre preference.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=Zwiegenhaft /><ref name="Nusbaum 199–204">{{cite journal|last=Nusbaum|first=E. C.|author2=Silvia, P. J. |title=Shivers and Timbres: Personality and the Experience of Chills From Music|journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science|date=7 October 2010|volume=2|issue=2|pages=199–204|doi=10.1177/1948550610386810|s2cid=54188929|url=https://libres.uncg.edu/ir/uncg/f/P_Silvia_Shivers_2011.pdf}}</ref> In general, those rated high in openness to experience prefer music categorized as more complex and novel, such as classical, jazz, and eclecticism,<ref>{{cite journal|last=Dunn|first=Peter G. |author2=de Ruyter, Boris |author3=Bouwhuis, Don G. |title=Toward a better understanding of the relation between music preference, listening behavior, and personality|journal=Psychology of Music|date=16 March 2011|volume=40|issue=4|pages=411–428|doi=10.1177/0305735610388897|s2cid=54736250 }}</ref> as well as intense and rebellious music.<ref name="rentfrow goldberg levitin"/><ref name="Langmeyer rudan tarnai" /><ref name=Zwiegenhaft /><ref name="Brown 18">{{cite journal|last=Brown|first=R. A.|title=Music preferences and personality among Japanese university students|journal=International Journal of Psychology|date=1 August 2012|volume=47|issue=4|pages=259–268|doi=10.1080/00207594.2011.631544|pmid=22248342}}</ref> In the study, reflective and complex genres included classical, blues, jazz, and folk music, while intense and rebellious genres included rock, alternative, and heavy metal music.<ref name="Langmeyer rudan tarnai" /> One of the facets of openness to experience is aesthetic appreciation, which is how researchers generally explain the high positive correlation between openness and liking complex music.<ref name="premuzic fagan furnham"/>

One study looking at how personality traits affect music-induced emotion found that, of all the traits, openness to experience was the best predictor of higher emotionally intense reactions to sad and slow music. The most common feelings described from [[sad music]] were nostalgia, peacefulness, and wonder, and openness to experience correlated positively with all these feelings.<ref name="Vuoskoski thompson">{{cite journal|last=Vuoskoski|first=Jonna K.|author2=Thompson, William F. |author3=McIlwain, Doris |author4=Eerola, Tuomas |title=Who enjoys listening to sad music and why?|journal=Music Perception|date=February 2012|volume=29|issue=3|pages=311–317|doi=10.1525/mp.2012.29.3.311|s2cid=145526250 |url=http://urn.fi/URN:NBN:fi:jyu-201601191144}}</ref> Sad music has also been theorized to allow for greater experience of aesthetic experiences and beauty.<ref name="Vuoskoski thompson" /> Furthermore, individuals scoring high on openness to experience show a preference for diverse musical styles, but do not prefer popular forms of contemporary music, indicating that there are limits to this openness.<ref name="Rawlings ciancarelli" /> However, this is only true up to a certain point, as another study looked at music's ability to produce "chills" in the listeners. Although this study found that openness was the best predictor of genre preference, there is no way to use openness to experience to predict whether one will get chills from music or not. Instead, the only measure for that was frequency of listening to music and the self-designated value of the importance of music in one's life.<ref name="Nusbaum 199–204"/>

Another study examined how openness to experience and frequency of listening are related and how they affect music preference. While listening to classical music excerpts, those rated high in openness tended to decrease in liking music faster during repeated listenings, as opposed to those scoring low in openness, who tended to like music more with repeated plays. This suggests novelty in music is an important quality for people high in openness to experience.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Hunter|first=Patrick G|author2=Schellenberg, Glen E. |title=Interactive effects of personality and frequency of exposure on liking for music|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|date=20 October 2010|volume=50|pages=175–179|doi=10.1016/j.paid.2010.09.021|issue=2}}</ref>

One study had people take a personality test before and after listening to classical music with and without written lyrics in front of them. Music both with and without lyrics showed some effect on people's self-reported personality traits, most significantly in terms of openness to experience, which showed a significant increase.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Djikic|first=Maja|title=The effect of music and lyrics on personality|journal=Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts|date=August 2011|volume=5|issue=3|pages=237–240|doi=10.1037/a0022313}}</ref> Instead of personality affecting music preference, here classical music altered the assessment of their own personalities and made people assess themselves as more open to experience.

Openness to experience is also positively correlated with frequency of intellectual or cognitive use of music, such as analyzing complex musical compositions.<ref name="premuzic fagan furnham" /><ref name="Premuzic Swami Furnham Maakip" /><ref name="Rentfrow gosling 2003" /><ref name="premuzic furnham freixanet muro"/> Furthermore, individuals more open to experience prefer a greater number of melodic themes in a work of music.<ref name="Steele young"/><ref name=Kopacz />

==== Conscientiousness ====
[[Conscientiousness]] is negatively correlated with intense and rebellious music, such as rock and heavy metal music.<ref name="Langmeyer rudan tarnai" /> While previous studies have found an association between conscientiousness and emotional regulation, these results do not apply cross-culturally—specifically, researchers did not find this association in Malaysia.<ref name="Premuzic Swami Furnham Maakip" />

==== Extroversion ====

Extroversion is another good predictor of music genre preference and music use. Energetic extroverts have been linked to preferences in happy, upbeat and conventional music, as well as energetic and rhythmic music, such as rap, hip hop, soul, electronic, and dance music.<ref name="premuzic fagan furnham" /><ref name="Langmeyer rudan tarnai" /> Additionally, extroverts tend to listen to music more and have background music present in their lives more often.<ref name="premuzic fagan furnham"/> One study compared introverts and extroverts to see who would be more easily distracted by background music with and without lyrics. It was assumed that since extroverts listen to background music more they would be able to tune it out better, but that was proved untrue. No matter how much music people listen to they are still equally affected and distracted by music with lyrics.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Avila|first=Christina |author2=Furnham, Adrian |author3=McClelland, Alastair|title=The influence of distracting familiar vocal music on cognitive performance of introverts and extraverts|journal=Psychology of Music|date=9 November 2011|volume=40|issue=1|pages=84–93|doi=10.1177/0305735611422672|s2cid=145340833 }}</ref> Another study found that extroverts liked more types of music compared to those who are introverts. It was found that extroverted individuals preferred popular/rock music compared to introverted individuals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pearson |first1=Jodi L |last2=Dollinger |first2=Stephen J |date=April 2004 |title=Music preference correlates of Jungian types |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0191886903001685 |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |language=en |volume=36 |issue=5 |pages=1005–1008 |doi=10.1016/S0191-8869(03)00168-5|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Cheerful music with fast tempos, many melodic themes, and vocals are also preferred by extroverts.<ref name="Rentfrow gosling 2003" /><ref name=Kopacz /><ref name="Brown 18" /> They are more likely than others to listen to music in the background while doing other activities, such as running, being with friends, or studying.<ref name="premuzic furnham freixanet muro"/><ref name="Premuzic Swami Furnham Maakip" /><ref name="premuzic fagan furnham" /> This group also tends to use music to counter the monotony of everyday tasks, such as ironing.<ref name="Premuzic Swami Furnham Maakip" /> In a Turkish study, researchers found that extroverts preferred rock, pop, and rap because these genres facilitated dance and movement.

Another study examined music teachers and music therapists, assuming that people who like and study music would be more extroverted. The results showed that music teachers were definitely higher in extroversion than the general public. Music therapists were also higher on extroversion than introversion, though they scored significantly lower than the teachers.<ref name="Steele young">{{cite journal|last=Steele|first=Anita Louise|author2=Young, Sylvester |title=A descriptive study of Myers-Briggs personality types of professional music educators and music therapists with comparisons to undergraduate majors|journal=Journal of Music Therapy|date=Spring 2011|volume=48|issue=1|pages=55–73|pmid=21866713|doi=10.1093/jmt/48.1.55}}</ref> Differences can probably be attributed to teaching being a profession more dependent on extroversion.

==== Agreeableness ====

Agreeable individuals preferred upbeat and conventional music.<ref name="Langmeyer rudan tarnai" /> Agreeableness has also been shown to correlate with more easy-going, relaxed music preferences.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Ruth|first1=Nicolas|last2=Müllensiefen|first2=Daniel|date=December 2020|title=Associations between music preferences and personality in female secondary school students.|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/344450006|journal=Psychomusicology: Music, Mind and Brain|volume=30|issue=4|pages=202–211|doi=10.1037/pmu0000267|s2cid=222113433}}</ref> Additionally, listeners with high agreeableness displayed an intense emotional response to music which they had never before listened to.<ref name="Ladinig schellenberg" /> Agreeableness is also a good predictor of the emotional intensity experienced from all types of music, both positive and negative. Those scoring high in agreeableness tend to have more intense emotional reactions to all types of music.<ref name="Ladinig schellenberg"/>

==== Neuroticism ====

The more neurotic a person is, the more likely they are to listen to intense and rebellious music (such as alternative, rock and heavy metal).<ref name="Langmeyer rudan tarnai" /><ref>{{cite journal|last=Greenberg|first=D.M |author2=Wride, S.J |author3=Snowden, D.A |author4=Spathis, D |author5=Potter, J |author6=Rentfrow, P.J |title=Universals and Variations in Music Preference: A study of preferential reaction to Western music in 53 countries |url= https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2022-29920-001 |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|date=February 2022|volume=122|issue=2|pages=286–309|doi=10.1037/pspp0000397|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Additionally, neuroticism is positively correlated with emotional use of music.<ref name="premuzic fagan furnham" /><ref name="premuzic furnham freixanet muro" /> Those who scored high in neuroticism were more likely to report use of music for emotional regulation and experience higher intensity of emotional affect, especially negative emotion.<ref name="premuzic fagan furnham" /><ref name="Premuzic Swami Furnham Maakip" />

==Individual and situational influences on musical preferences==
Situations have been shown to influence individual's preferences for certain types of music. Participants in a study from 1996 provided information about what music they would prefer to listen to in given situations, and indicated that the situation greatly determined their musical preferences. For example, melancholic situations called for sad and moody music, while an arousal situation would call for loud, strong rhythm, invigorating music.<ref name="north hargreaves 2">{{cite journal|last=North|first=Adrian|author2=Hargreaves, David |title=Situational influences on reported musical preference|journal=Psychomusicology|year=1996|volume=15|issue=1–2|pages=30–45|doi=10.1037/h0094081}}</ref>

===Gender===

Women are more likely than men to respond to music in a more emotional way.<ref name="premuzic furnham freixanet muro"/> Furthermore, women prefer popular music more than men.<ref name="Rawlings ciancarelli">{{cite journal|last=Rawlings|first=D.|author2=Ciancarelli, V. |title=Music Preference and the Five-Factor Model of the NEO Personality Inventory|journal=Psychology of Music|date=1 October 1997|volume=25|issue=2|pages=120–132|doi=10.1177/0305735697252003|s2cid=145601419}}</ref> In a study of personality and gender in preference for exaggerated bass in music, researchers found that men demonstrated more of a preference for bassy music than women. This preference for bassy music is also correlated with [[Antisocial personality disorder|antisocial]] and [[borderline personality disorder|borderline]] personalities.<ref name="McCown Keiser Mulhearn Williamson">{{cite journal|last=McCown|first=William|author2=Keiser, Ross |author3=Mulhearn, Shea |author4= Williamson, David |title=The role of personality and gender in preference for exaggerated bass in music|journal=Personality and Individual Differences|year=1997|volume=23|issue=4|pages=543–547 |doi=10.1016/s0191-8869(97)00085-8}}</ref>

=== Culture ===
Research has shown culture can influence musical preferences. Humans tend to enjoy culturally similar music more than culturally atypical music.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last1=McDermott|first1=Josh|last2=Schultz|first2=Alan|date=July 13, 2016|title=Indifference to dissonance in native Amazonians reveals cultural variation in music perception|journal=Nature|volume=535|issue=7613|pages=547–550|doi=10.1038/nature18635|pmid=27409816|bibcode=2016Natur.535..547M|s2cid=205249764|hdl=10533/238351|hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal|last1=Stupacher|first1=Jan|last2=Wood|first2=Guilherme|date=July 2018|title=Effects of cultural background and musical preference on affective social entrainment with music|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/327981750|journal=Proceedings of ICMPC15/ESCOM10|pages=438–441}}</ref> In a study conducted by McDermott and Schultz (2016), participants from three regions in Bolivia (La Paz, San Borja, and Santa Maria) and the United States were evaluated for differences in music preferences.<ref name=":1" /> The participants from the United States were divided into two groups, with one group possessing at least two years of experience playing an instrument and the other group having a maximum of one year of experience. Participants from Bolivia were divided into groups based on their region, with each region having experienced varying levels of exposure to Western culture. The group of participants from Santa Maria, the Tsimane, were of specific interest due to the substantial differences between their music and Western music in addition to their relative isolation from Western cultural influences.<ref name=":1" /> The participants were exposed to traditionally consonant and dissonant sounds from Western music and asked to rate how pleasant they found the sound. Results indicated that Americans, particularly trained musicians, demonstrated the strongest preference for consonances in their music.<ref name=":1" /> The groups from La Paz and San Borja also demonstrated some preference for [[Consonance and dissonance|consonance]]. However, the Tsimane enjoyed both consonant and dissonant chords equally, suggesting that consonance preferences in music are developed through cultural exposure and humans have no innate preferences for certain harmonies or chords.<ref name=":1" />

Culture changes how music is perceived by an individual, and alters affective response to music, particularly in a social context.<ref name=":2" /> People feel more connected with one another when listening to culturally typical music than when they listen to culturally atypical music. Previous research has suggested that listening to familiar music might also aid in establishing more significant movement predictability, which can result in more movement synchronicity between individuals, further amplifying feelings of group connection and enjoyment of music.<ref name=":2" /> Culture also affects a person's ability to remember music.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal|last1=Demorest|first1=Steven M.|last2=Morrison|first2=Steven J.|date=June 1, 2016|title=The influence of contextual cues on cultural bias in music memory.|journal=Music Perception|volume=33|issue=5|pages=590–600|doi=10.1525/mp.2016.33.5.590|jstor=26417336}}</ref> A study on cross-cultural music memory was conducted by Demorest and Morrison (2016). In the study, Western participants were played six musical excerpts. Three of the excerpts were from Turkish classical music, and the other three were from Western classical music. The music was presented in three different musical contexts (original, monophonic and isochronous).Once all of the excerpts were played, participants received a memory test. The memory test results indicate that participant memory was consistently better at recalling in-cultural music than unfamiliar music.<ref name=":4" />

===Age===

Age is a strong factor in determining music preference.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{cite journal|last=Barret|first=Frederick S.|author2=Grimm, Kevin J. |author3=Robins, Richard W. |author4=Wildschut, Tim |author5=Constantine, Sedikides |author6=Janata, Petr |title=Music-evoked nostalgia: Affect, memory, and personality|journal=Emotion|date=June 2010|volume=10|issue=3|pages=390–403|doi=10.1037/a0019006|pmid=20515227|s2cid=17454039 }}</ref> There is also evidence that preferences and opinions toward music can change with age.<ref name=":0" /><ref name="crowther">{{cite journal|last=Crowther|first=R|author2=Durkin, K |title=Sex- and age-related differences in the musical behavior, interests and attitudes towards music of 232 secondary school students|journal=Educational Studies|year=1982|volume=8|issue=2|pages=131–139|doi=10.1080/0305569820080206}}</ref> In a Canadian study concerning how adolescent music preferences relate to personality, researchers found that adolescents who preferred heavy music demonstrated low self-esteem, higher levels of discomfort within the family, and tended to feel rejected by others. Adolescents who preferred light music were preoccupied with doing the proper thing, and had difficulty balancing independence with dependence. Adolescents who had eclectic music preferences had less difficulty negotiating adolescence, and were flexible using music according to mood and particular needs at the time.<ref name="schwartz fouts g">{{cite journal|last=Schwartz|first=Kelly|author2=Fouts, Gregory |title=Music preferences, personality style, and developmental issues of adolescents|journal=Journal of Youth and Adolescence|year=2003|volume=32|issue=3|pages=205–213|doi=10.1023/a:1022547520656|s2cid=41849910}}</ref>

===Self views===

Music preferences can also be influenced by how the individual wants to be perceived, especially in males.<ref name="premuzic fagan furnham" /> Music preferences could be used to make self-directed identity claims. Individuals might select styles of music that reinforce their self-views. For examples, individuals with a conservative self-view preferred conventional styles of music, while individuals with an athletic self view preferred vigorous music.<ref name="Rentfrow gosling 2003" /> Individuals will unconsciously push perceptions into their environments, music makes this evident. In a 1953 study, Cattell and Anderson began the process of determining musical preference through unconscious traits.<ref name="rentfrow goldberg levitin">{{Cite journal |last1=Rentfrow |first1=Peter J.|last2=Goldberg|first2=Lewis R.|last3=Levitin|first3=Daniel J.|date=June 2011|title=The Structure of Musical Preferences: A Five-Factor Model|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=100|issue=6|pages=1139–1157 |doi=10.1037/a0022406 |issn=0022-3514 |pmc=3138530 |pmid=21299309}}</ref> While their findings were inconclusive, it created a research basis for later cases. Music is a way similar to diet and physique to outwardly express internal characteristics. Rentfrow and Gosling found through their study of the seven domains that for many, music was placed quite high in the rankings.<ref name="Rentfrow gosling 2003"/>

===Mood===

Active mood is another factor that affects music preference. Generally whether people are in a good or bad mood when they hear music affects how they feel about the type of music and also their emotional response.<ref name="Vuoskoski" /> On that line of thinking, aggression has been shown to improve creativity and emotional intensity derived from music. People with aggressive disorders find music to be a powerful emotional outlet.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Pool |first=Jonathan|author2=Odell-Miller, Helen |title=Aggression in music therapy and its role in creativity with reference to personality disorder|journal=The Arts in Psychotherapy|year=2011|volume=38|pages=169–177|doi=10.1016/j.aip.2011.04.003|issue=3}}</ref> Depression also impacts perceptions and emotional responses generated from music. Depressed individuals rate their music-associated episodic memories lower than non-depressed individuals.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Sakka|first1=Laura|last2=Juslin|first2=Patrik|date=October 10, 2017|title=Emotional reactions to music in depressed individuals|journal=Psychology of Music|volume=46|issue=6 |pages=862–880 |doi=10.1177/0305735617730425 |s2cid=148886910}}</ref> Additionally, the value people put on music and frequency of listening affects their reactions to it. If people listen to a certain type of music and add emotional experience to songs or a genre in general, this increases the likelihood of enjoying the music and being emotionally affected by it.<ref name="Nusbaum 199–204" /> This helps explain why many people might have strong reactions to music their parents listened to frequently when they were children.

=== Movement ===
Research suggests that body movement may play a role in determining musical tastes.<ref name=":03">{{Cite journal|last1=Weigelt|first1=Oliver |last2=Walther |first2=Eva|date=February 2011|title=Music is in the Muscle: How Embodied Cognition May Influence Music Preferences|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/259730932|journal=Music Perception|volume=28|issue=3|pages=297–306|doi=10.1525/mp.2011.28.3.297}}</ref> Body movement can create either positive or negative feelings towards music depending on if the movement is associated with a positive or negative affective state. A study conducted by Weigelt and Walther (2011) demonstrated that performing a positive physical movement while listening to a song led to an increased preference rating for that song compared to a negative physical movement. Movements associated with smiling muscles (both positive or negative) were found to have the largest effect on musical preference.<ref name=":03" />

===Productiveness===
If someone is listening to music with the ultimate goal of completing a task, their musical preference is greatly increased. The more a genre of music increases one's productiveness, the more the individual will gravitate toward that genre to complete future tasks.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Schäfer |first=Thomas |date=2016-03-17|title=The Goals and Effects of Music Listening and Their Relationship to the Strength of Music Preference|journal=PLOS ONE |language=en|volume=11|issue=3|article-number=e0151634 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0151634|issn=1932-6203 |pmc=4795651|pmid=26985998 |bibcode=2016PLoSO..1151634S|doi-access=free}}</ref>

In turn, music can increase focus in some. It can help your brain interpret information and gain a better understanding of new things more easily. Music can engage the brain in many different ways, whether that be making one more attentive, focused, increased concentration etc.<ref>{{Cite journal|date=2007-08-02|title=Neural Dynamics of Event Segmentation in Music: Converging Evidence for Dissociable Ventral and Dorsal Networks|journal=Neuron|language=en|volume=55|issue=3|pages=521–532|doi=10.1016/j.neuron.2007.07.003|issn=0896-6273|last1=Sridharan|first1=Devarajan|last2=Levitin|first2=Daniel J.|last3=Chafe|first3=Chris H.|last4=Berger|first4=Jonathan|last5=Menon|first5=Vinod|pmid=17678862|s2cid=6846417|doi-access=free}}</ref>

According to a 2017 study, soft, fast music was concluded to have a positive effect on productiveness. Songs with lyrics are potentially more distracting than instrumental melodies and music with other modes or tempos could possibly evoke obstructive emotions for learning. Music can improve one's mood, create a positive mindset, reduce stress etc., this can directly correlate to learning improvements.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lehmann|first1=Janina A. M.|last2=Seufert|first2=Tina|date=2017|title=The Influence of Background Music on Learning in the Light of Different Theoretical Perspectives and the Role of Working Memory Capacity|journal=Frontiers in Psychology|language=en|volume=8|page=1902|doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2017.01902|pmid=29163283|pmc=5671572|issn=1664-1078|doi-access=free}}</ref>

===Season of the year===

Season of the year can also affect preferences. After reflecting upon fall or winter seasons, participants preferred reflective and complex music, whereas after reflecting upon summer or spring, participants preferred energetic and rhythmic music. In the same study, it was found that meaningful songs were preferred in the colder seasons (fall and winter) whereas dance songs are preferred in the warmer (spring and summer) seasons. However, "pop" music seems to have a universal appeal, despite the season.<ref name="Pettijohn">{{cite journal|last=Pettijohn|first=Terry F.|author2=Williams, Greg M.|author3=Carter, Tiffany C.|date=26 November 2010|title=Music for the Seasons: Seasonal Music Preferences in College Students|journal=Current Psychology|volume=29|issue=4|pages=328–345|doi=10.1007/s12144-010-9092-8|s2cid=56037002}}</ref>

=== Familiarity ===
Familiarity and complexity both have interesting effects on musical preferences. As seen in other types of artistic media, an inverted U relationship is apparent when relating subjective complexity on liking music excerpts. Individuals like complexity to a certain degree, then begin to dislike the music when complexity gets too high. Furthermore, there is a clear positive monotonic relationship between familiarity and liking of music.<ref name="north hargreaves">{{cite journal|last=North|first=Adrian|author2=Hargreaves, David|year=1995|title=Subjective complexity, familiarity, and liking for popular music|journal=Psychomusicology|volume=14|issue=1–2|pages=77–93|doi=10.1037/h0094090}}</ref>
